speaker: interviewer
text: Eva Shane, tape five. Well, you've shared your story with us. And, and I wanted to ask you at this time how your Holocaust experience is reflected in your current religious observance in any way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say there has been a-- it has caused a change in the way I view religion, definitely. I was raised orthodox, and I believed everything very strictly. Never asked any questions about it. Now, I definitely look at it differently. I see religion more as something that we, as human, need for moral support. But I don't really take it literally like I used to. We need some spirituality. We need-- we-- If we feel insecure, we feel afraid, we have to grab onto something. That's why we need religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: But believing, literally, in what religion teaches us and wants us to believe? No. That's absolutely impossible. Because having gone through the Holocaust, something so horrible, and asking for an answer-- there is no answer. So it can't be the way we're being told.

speaker: interviewee
text: I consider religion more as a symbol for what is out there, and we will never know. And that is too big and too distant for our mind to grasp. So we need something to hang on to, and that's what it-- what it's accomplished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I wanted to ask you, and I'm going back a little bit. But in the town you lived in, in France, when you had the false identity and, and the papers, we never talked about practicing Judaism behind the doors on the farm. And I'm curious as to-- if any of that went on during that time in your own family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. I remember we had prayer books. And of course, there was no synagogue or anything like that. But yeah, Yom Kippur, I remember my father had his [NON-ENGLISH], and we would say the prayers. He would go through the [INAUDIBLE], and he would say the prayers in the morning. And my father was a believer. I mean, nothing would have changed his, his, his way he looked at-- I mean, to him, this was the absolute truth. He never-- he never questioned anything. He never-- it's too bad that I can't discuss it with him. To me, it brought a, a definite change in the way I approached it. But it probably gave him a lot of comfort, moral comfort and spiritual comfort. And I think that's one of the great things that religion accomplishes to many people. So it does a lot of good, and it serves a purpose, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, 50 years after the liberation, is there any message you'd like to leave to your children, 50 years after the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's easy to say never again, it will never happen. Unfortunately, as you watch human behavior, and even international events, unfortunately, you have great doubts about this. That, that human-- human behavior will ever change for the better. But luckily, there's always a few good ones. And I think those are the ones to look at, and make you forget the rest, the horror, you know, of the masses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fight cruelty and injustice as much as you can. Every little bit helps, by any means you can. It's about all we can do. And try to protect the weak and the helpless. And hope for the best, you know? Well, at this time, I would like to thank you so much for sharing your incredible story. It's been wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Eva.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] This is my father as a student, a university student in Germany. He went to Marburg University. So I don't know what year that was taken, but he was born in 1888. So he must be about 20. This is my mother before she was-- probably shortly before she got married. She was born '99, so it must be around 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what's your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna. Her maiden name was Anna Komaltzich. Here's a photo of my parents, either young married or before they got married. They're probably young married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in Germany. No, that's not Germany, but maybe they were on vacation somewhere. That I couldn't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date? Do you know about the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would-- I don't know. No. This was taken, as far as I know, during our trip from Germany to Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were leaving Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother holding us. And next to her is a niece of my father's, a cousin of mine who lived in Frankfurt. And she was staying with us when we left, and she stayed with us many years afterwards in Belgium. This is taken in Belgium, in Antwerp, shortly after we immigrated, obviously.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, again, my, my cousin, me, and my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year that you had immigrated to Belgium, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '33. So it must be around '33, '34 at the most. Yeah. This is graduation day, Antwerp. I must be about 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the picture with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's about 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a year older than me, so I would guess I'm 10 and he's 11. Here's my fake identity card that was made and given to us by the mayor of that village in France. Different name. My real name was Eva Shane-- Eva Cohn. [LAUGHS] Sorry. And they changed it into Jacqueline Moulin very French sounding name. And this also contributed to save our life at the time. Here's the house where we lived in France. Very old building. No plumbing, no running water. Nothing very elegant, but it served the purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you live here? What years did you live here with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- Till '43. We lived in one place two years, and the other about two years also, which was about the same type of building. I had to get our water from a well, you know, like, half a block away. And it was very primitive, but it served the purpose. That's my father, you know, as a farmer in front of our house. Like we would be integrated, you know, in farm life and work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the same house, in the same place. Same house that we just saw in France. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably again '42, '43. You know, doing hard manual work and helping farmers. And also growing our own vegetables. That was part of our job too. So we had food. Here's me with one of a farmer neighbors on his cart, helping him doing I don't remember what. [LAUGHS] But we always had things to do. This is the convent, the Carmelite Convent, where I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this exactly? The city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] That was also in France. It was called-- the village was called Saint-Martin-Belle-Roche. And very picturesque place. Wine country. A lot of vineyards in the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the years you went here were?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was between '41 and '44. Here is a recent picture of me and Bob, my husband. It was taken at Junaluska Lake, North Carolina recently. My two daughters. Katherine, we name her Kathy, and Jacqueline. We name her Jackie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- Which side is Kathy on, as we're looking at it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The left is Kathy, Katherine. And the right is Jacqueline, my firstborn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are 30 and 27.
